text: In the early days of email, researchers at IBM observed that the average response time to a message was around two days. By 2019, tracking studies showed the median reply arrived within ninety seconds of receipt. This compression did not happen because senders demanded faster replies; it happened because the medium itself, by making instant response frictionless, created a new social norm. Historian Joanne McNeil calls this 'ratchet pressure': once a faster response is possible, the slower one becomes a slight, and politeness compels the upgrade. The result is a communication culture that has optimized for speed at the expense of substance.
question: According to the passage, what mechanism drove the shift from two-day to ninety-second email response times?

options.text: Corporate policies mandating faster customer service response windows
The frictionlessness of instant reply creating a social norm that made slower responses feel rude
Senders explicitly requesting faster replies in their messages
Smartphone notifications trained users to associate speed with professionalism
